value.title: Chapter One: Closing Time
paragraphs: The last shoppers drifted toward the exits like sleepwalkers being gently steered. Plastic bags swung at their sides, their faces pale under fluorescent light that made everyone look a little unfinished. A janitor pushed a wide mop bucket through the concourse, its wheels squealing in brief protest before settling back into the mall’s steady hum.

Caleb Shaw watched the flow from behind the security desk near the central fountain, where coins glinted in shallow water that hadn’t been cleaned in weeks. He wore the navy blazer the job required, but the tie was already loosened, and his badge sat slightly crooked from habit. New uniforms always felt like someone else’s skin.

“Ten minutes,” his supervisor said, voice crackling through the handheld radio. Frankie had a smoker’s rasp and the tone of a man who’d seen the same night repeat itself for years. “Once the gates come down, it’s you and the cameras.”

Caleb lifted the radio closer. “Copy,” he said. He tried to sound steady, tried to sound like this wasn’t his first solo night at Meridian. He’d worked smaller places—warehouse lots, a shuttered office building—nothing with this many corridors, this many blank storefronts, this much polished space designed for crowds.

Meridian Mall sat on the edge of town, built during the optimistic years when people thought indoor plazas could replace main streets. In daylight it felt ordinary, even cheerful in a tired way. At night, it became a maze of glossy tiles and reflective glass, a place that seemed to wait for something to return.

Overhead, the public-address system cleared its throat with a soft pop. “Attention Meridian guests,” the recorded voice said, warm and artificial. “The time is now nine-fifty. Meridian Mall will be closing in ten minutes. Please make your final selections and proceed to the nearest exit.”
The voice belonged to a woman Caleb had never met and yet heard again and again. Tonight, the recording sounded slightly slower, as if the tape were dragging. He told himself it was the speakers.

The main doors slid shut in pairs as the last people left, leaving only the dull, private click of locks. One by one, store shutters rolled down like metal eyelids. The concourse dimmed as kiosks and neon signs switched off, their colors collapsing into the gray-green of emergency lighting.

Caleb did his first round the way Frankie had taught him: clockwise, main level first, then upper. He carried a keyring heavy enough to bruise a pocket, a small flashlight, and a clipboard with a checklist that felt like a joke in a place this big. The mall had cameras, but patrols were still expected—some old philosophy of security that believed presence mattered.

His shoes made a soft tack-tack on tile. It echoed longer than it should have, the sound traveling ahead of him like an announcement. He passed the closed arcade, its gate latticed and dark, the silhouettes of machines inside forming a little city of sleeping screens. In the food court, chairs were stacked on tables like folded insects, and the smell of fryer oil clung to the air as if it had seeped into the walls.

Near the fountain, Caleb paused to watch the water. The pumps had been turned down for the night, but the surface still trembled as if something beneath it was breathing. He leaned closer, then straightened, annoyed with himself. Fluorescent light played tricks on moving water. That was all.

His radio hissed once, then went quiet. He glanced at it, thumbed the button, and heard only static. Frankie had warned him the building ate signals in certain spots. “Dead zones,” he’d called them, with a half grin that didn’t reach his eyes.

At the far end of the concourse, the corridor to the north wing waited in shadow. Caleb had seen it during the day—anchor stores, a long line of boutiques, a service hallway behind them. Now, with the lights reduced, that corridor looked longer, narrower, more deliberate.

Frankie’s voice came back in Caleb’s memory: *Don’t linger up there. North wing sensors are finicky. Doors stick. If something pings, check it and move on.* He’d said it like advice. He’d looked at Caleb like a warning.

Caleb started down the corridor anyway, telling himself a job was a job. He swept his flashlight beam across store gates, over mannequins frozen in bright outfits that made no sense without customers. Their faces were blank ovals. Their hands held invisible waists. The beam caught them in slices, making them appear to turn when he moved.

Halfway down, his gut tightened for no good reason. The air felt cooler here, not with a draft, but with the sensation of stepping into someone else’s quiet. Caleb slowed, listening. The mall’s background hum was still present, yet thinner, as if the building were holding its breath.

He reached a directory kiosk—one of those backlit maps with bright “YOU ARE HERE” arrows. The screen was dark for the night, but the printed map beneath the glass was still visible. Caleb glanced at it out of habit.
The north wing on the map ended at the old department store. A clean line. A final wall.
In front of him, the corridor continued past that point.

It wasn’t subtle. The line of tile stretched on into dimness, the ceiling lights repeating in a pattern that suggested more mall than the map allowed. Caleb blinked, looked down again, then up, waiting for his brain to correct itself.
His radio popped. Not Frankie. A thin burst of breath and a sound like a microphone being moved.
“Frankie?” Caleb asked. He pressed the button, held it down a second too long, then released. The static returned, heavier now, as if it had gained texture.

Something moved near the ceiling—just a flicker, like a light stuttering. Caleb raised his flashlight and followed the beam upward. The fixtures were steady. The only motion was his own hand trembling a little.
He told himself to turn around, to do the sane thing and finish the round where the lights were brighter. His feet didn’t obey right away. Curiosity made a kind of gravity.

A faint sound drifted from ahead. Not footsteps. More like the soft shush of cloth against tile, the whisper of movement that didn’t want to be caught. Caleb’s mouth went dry. He started walking again, slower, careful with each step, as if the floor might punish him for noise.

The corridor widened into a small junction he didn’t recognize, where a service door sat flush with the wall. It was painted the same beige as everything else and should have been forgettable. Caleb stared at it because the sign mounted above the handle looked new.
**SUBLEVEL ACCESS — AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL ONLY**
The mall didn’t have a sublevel. Not according to Frankie. Not according to the map. Caleb leaned closer and saw fresh screws in the sign’s corners, metal bright against the worn paint.

His radio crackled again, and this time the sound carried shape—someone trying to speak through a mouthful of static. Caleb held the device close to his ear, heart thudding hard enough to make his fingertips numb.
A voice slipped through, faint but unmistakable, pitched low as if afraid of being overheard.
It was his own.
“Caleb,” it said, distorted, stretched like tape. “Don’t—don’t open—”
The transmission cut off with a sharp click.
Behind him, in the main concourse, the mall’s public-address system came alive without warning. No preamble. No polite chime.
“Attention Meridian guests,” the recorded woman’s voice said, and for the first time Caleb heard something wrong in it—an inflection that didn’t belong to a loop. “The time is now…”
There was a pause, long enough for Caleb to count two breaths.
“…now.”
The lights above the corridor flickered. The service door’s handle shifted with a soft metallic tick, as if someone on the other side had tested it.

Caleb’s hand tightened around the keyring. The keys bit into his palm. He stood facing the door that shouldn’t exist, listening to the mall’s vast silence rearrange itself around him, and understood with sudden clarity that the building was no longer simply empty.

scenes.title: Closing Shift Begins
scenes.summary: Caleb watches the closing mall from the security desk, noting the drifting shoppers and his own uneasy uniform.

scenes.title: Mall Secures for Night
scenes.summary: The mall’s announcement sounds, doors lock, and lights dim as the building empties for the night.
paragraphs: Meridian Mall sat on the edge of town, built during the optimistic years when people thought indoor plazas could replace main streets. In daylight it felt ordinary, even cheerful in a tired way. At night, it became a maze of glossy tiles and reflective glass, a place that seemed to wait for something to return.

The voice belonged to a woman Caleb had never met and yet heard again and again. Tonight, the recording sounded slightly slower, as if the tape were dragging. He told himself it was the speakers.

The main doors slid shut in pairs as the last people left, leaving only the dull, private click of locks. One by one, store shutters rolled down like metal eyelids. The concourse dimmed as kiosks and neon signs switched off, their colors collapsing into the gray-green of emergency lighting.

scenes.title: First Patrol Loop
scenes.summary: Caleb starts his patrol, walking through the food court and fountain area while his radio intermittently dies.

He carried a keyring heavy enough to bruise a pocket, a small flashlight, and a clipboard with a checklist that felt like a joke in a place this big. In the food court, chairs were stacked on tables like folded insects, and the smell of fryer oil clung to the air as if it had seeped into the walls.

scenes.title: Hidden Door and Echo
scenes.summary: Strange radio noises lead Caleb to a hidden service door marked “SUBLEVEL ACCESS,” where he hears his own voice warning him to stay back.

“Frankie?” Caleb asked. He pressed the button, held it down a second too long, then released. The static returned, heavier now, as if it had gained texture.

It was his own.
“Caleb,” it said, distorted, stretched like tape. “Don’t—don’t open—”
The transmission cut off with a sharp click.

scenes.title: The Building Awakes
scenes.summary: The PA announces again, lights flicker, the door handle clicks, and Caleb realizes the mall is no longer empty.
paragraphs: Behind him, in the main concourse, the mall’s public-address system came alive without warning. No preamble. No polite chime.
“Attention Meridian guests,” the recorded woman’s voice said, and for the first time Caleb heard something wrong in it—an inflection that didn’t belong to a loop. “The time is now…”

“…now.”
The lights above the corridor flickered. The service door’s handle shifted with a soft metallic tick, as if someone on the other side had tested it.

value.title: Hidden Door and Echo

value.summary: The PA announces again, lights flicker, the door handle clicks, and Caleb realizes the mall is no longer empty.
value.title: The Building Awakes

summary.value: Caleb Shaw, a rookie security guard at the newly‑closed Meridian Mall, begins his first solo night shift as the last shoppers leave and the building’s lights dim, following Frankie’s radio briefing and a prescribed patrol route through the empty corridors. While checking the north‑wing hallway, he notices a discrepancy between the printed directory and the actual layout, leading him to a hidden service door marked “SUBLEVEL ACCESS—AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL ONLY” that shouldn’t exist. As he examines the door, his radio crackles with a distorted playback of his own voice warning him not to open it, and the mall’s PA system suddenly announces the closing time with an eerie inflection. The lights flicker, the door’s handle clicks, and Caleb realizes the silent mall is awakening around him, hinting at a hidden space and an unseen presence he must now confront.

text.value:
# After Hours at Meridian Mall

## Chapter One: Closing Time

The last shoppers drifted toward the exits like sleepwalkers being gently steered. Plastic bags swung at their sides, their faces pale under fluorescent light that made everyone look a little unfinished. A janitor pushed a wide mop bucket through the concourse, its wheels squealing in brief protest before settling back into the mall’s steady hum.

He’d worked smaller places—warehouse lots, a shuttered office building—nothing with this many corridors, this many blank storefronts, this much polished space designed for crowds.

At night, it became a maze of glossy tiles and reflective glass, a place that seemed to wait for something to return.

The voice belonged to a woman Caleb had never met and yet heard again and again. Tonight, the recording sounded slightly slower, as if the tape were dragging. He told himself it was the speakers.

He told himself to turn around, to do the sane thing and finish the round where the lights were brighter. His feet didn’t obey right away. Curiosity made a kind of gravity.

A faint sound drifted from ahead. Not footsteps. More like the soft shush of cloth against tile, the whisper of movement that didn’t want to be caught. Caleb’s mouth went dry. He started walking again, slower, careful with each step, as if the floor might punish him for noise.

The corridor widened into a small junction he didn’t recognize, where a service door sat flush with the wall. It was painted the same beige as everything else and should have been forgettable. Caleb stared at it because the sign mounted above the handle looked new.

**SUBLEVEL ACCESS — AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL ONLY**

The mall didn’t have a sublevel. Not according to Frankie. Not according to the map. Caleb leaned closer and saw fresh screws in the sign’s corners, metal bright against the worn paint.

His radio crackled again, and this time the sound carried shape—someone trying to speak through a mouthful of static. Caleb held the device close to his ear, heart thudding hard enough to make his fingertips numb.

A voice slipped through, faint but unmistakable, pitched low as if afraid of being overheard.

It was his own.

“Caleb,” it said, distorted, stretched like tape. “Don’t—don’t open—”

The transmission cut off with a sharp click.

Behind him, in the main concourse, the mall’s public-address system came alive without warning. No preamble. No polite chime.

“Attention Meridian guests,” the recorded woman’s voice said, and for the first time Caleb heard something wrong in it—an inflection that didn’t belong to a loop. “The time is now…”

There was a pause, long enough for Caleb to count two breaths.

“…now.”

The lights above the corridor flickered. The service door’s handle shifted with a soft metallic tick, as if someone on the other side had tested it.

Caleb’s hand tightened around the keyring. The keys bit into his palm. He stood facing the door that shouldn’t exist, listening to the mall’s vast silence rearrange itself around him, and understood with sudden clarity that the building was no longer simply empty.

## Chapter One: Closing Time

#### Paragraph 1
The last shoppers drifted toward the exits like sleepwalkers being gently steered. Plastic bags swung at their sides, their faces pale under fluorescent light that made everyone look a little unfinished. A janitor pushed a wide mop bucket through the concourse, its wheels squealing in brief protest before settling back into the mall’s steady hum.

#### Paragraph 2
Caleb Shaw watched the flow from behind the security desk near the central fountain, where coins glinted in shallow water that hadn’t been cleaned in weeks. He wore the navy blazer the job required, but the tie was already loosened, and his badge sat slightly crooked from habit. New uniforms always felt like someone else’s skin.

#### Paragraph 3
“Ten minutes,” his supervisor said, voice crackling through the handheld radio. Frankie had a smoker’s rasp and the tone of a man who’d seen the same night repeat itself for years. “Once the gates come down, it’s you and the cameras.”

#### Paragraph 4
Caleb lifted the radio closer. “Copy,” he said. He tried to sound steady, tried to sound like this wasn’t his first solo night at Meridian. He’d worked smaller places—warehouse lots, a shuttered office building—nothing with this many corridors, this many blank storefronts, this much polished space designed for crowds.

#### Paragraph 5

At night, it became a maze of glossy tiles and reflective glass, a place that seemed to wait for something to return.

#### Paragraph 7
The voice belonged to a woman Caleb had never met and yet heard again and again. Tonight, the recording sounded slightly slower, as if the tape were dragging. He told himself it was the speakers.

#### Paragraph 8
The main doors slid shut in pairs as the last people left, leaving only the dull, private click of locks. One by one, store shutters rolled down like metal eyelids. The concourse dimmed as kiosks and neon signs switched off, their colors collapsing into the gray-green of emergency lighting.

Caleb did his first round the way Frankie had taught him: clockwise, main level first, then upper. He carried a keyring heavy enough to bruise a pocket, a small flashlight, and a clipboard with a checklist that felt like a joke in a place this big. #### Paragraph 15
Caleb started down the corridor anyway, telling himself a job was a job. He swept his flashlight beam across store gates, over mannequins frozen in bright outfits that made no sense without customers. Their faces were blank ovals. Their hands held invisible waists. text_with_scenes.value:
# After Hours at Meridian Mall

## Chapter One: Closing Time

### Scene 1 Closing Shift Begins

The last shoppers drifted toward the exits like sleepwalkers being gently steered. Plastic bags swung at their sides, their faces pale under fluorescent light that made everyone look a little unfinished. A janitor pushed a wide mop bucket through the concourse, its wheels squealing in brief protest before settling back into the mall’s steady hum.

Caleb Shaw watched the flow from behind the security desk near the central fountain, where coins glinted in shallow water that hadn’t been cleaned in weeks. He wore the navy blazer the job required, but the tie was already loosened, and his badge sat slightly crooked from habit. New uniforms always felt like someone else’s skin.

### Scene 2 Radio Briefing

“Ten minutes,” his supervisor said, voice crackling through the handheld radio. Frankie had a smoker’s rasp and the tone of a man who’d seen the same night repeat itself for years. “Once the gates come down, it’s you and the cameras.”

Caleb lifted the radio closer. “Copy,” he said. He tried to sound steady, tried to sound like this wasn’t his first solo night at Meridian. He’d worked smaller places—warehouse lots, a shuttered office building—nothing with this many corridors, this many blank storefronts, this much polished space designed for crowds.

### Scene 3 Mall Secures for Night

Meridian Mall sat on the edge of town, built during the optimistic years when people thought indoor plazas could replace main streets. In daylight it felt ordinary, even cheerful in a tired way. At night, it became a maze of glossy tiles and reflective glass, a place that seemed to wait for something to return.

Overhead, the public-address system cleared its throat with a soft pop. “Attention Meridian guests,” the recorded voice said, warm and artificial. “The time is now nine-fifty. Meridian Mall will be closing in ten minutes. Please make your final selections and proceed to the nearest exit.”

The voice belonged to a woman Caleb had never met and yet heard again and again. Tonight, the recording sounded slightly slower, as if the tape were dragging. He told himself it was the speakers.

The main doors slid shut in pairs as the last people left, leaving only the dull, private click of locks. One by one, store shutters rolled down like metal eyelids. The concourse dimmed as kiosks and neon signs switched off, their colors collapsing into the gray-green of emergency lighting.

### Scene 4 First Patrol Loop

Caleb did his first round the way Frankie had taught him: clockwise, main level first, then upper. He carried a keyring heavy enough to bruise a pocket, a small flashlight, and a clipboard with a checklist that felt like a joke in a place this big. The mall had cameras, but patrols were still expected—some old philosophy of security that believed presence mattered.

His shoes made a soft tack-tack on tile. It echoed longer than it should have, the sound traveling ahead of him like an announcement. He passed the closed arcade, its gate latticed and dark, the silhouettes of machines inside forming a little city of sleeping screens. In the food court, chairs were stacked on tables like folded insects, and the smell of fryer oil clung to the air as if it had seeped into the walls.

Near the fountain, Caleb paused to watch the water. The pumps had been turned down for the night, but the surface still trembled as if something beneath it was breathing. He leaned closer, then straightened, annoyed with himself. Fluorescent light played tricks on moving water. That was all.

His radio hissed once, then went quiet. He glanced at it, thumbed the button, and heard only static. Frankie had warned him the building ate signals in certain spots. “Dead zones,” he’d called them, with a half grin that didn’t reach his eyes.

### Scene 5 North Wing Entrance

At the far end of the concourse, the corridor to the north wing waited in shadow. Caleb had seen it during the day—anchor stores, a long line of boutiques, a service hallway behind them. Now, with the lights reduced, that corridor looked longer, narrower, more deliberate.

Frankie’s voice came back in Caleb’s memory: *Don’t linger up there. North wing sensors are finicky. Doors stick. If something pings, check it and move on.* He’d said it like advice. He’d looked at Caleb like a warning.

Caleb started down the corridor anyway, telling himself a job was a job. He swept his flashlight beam across store gates, over mannequins frozen in bright outfits that made no sense without customers. Their faces were blank ovals. Their hands held invisible waists. The beam caught them in slices, making them appear to turn when he moved.

Halfway down, his gut tightened for no good reason. The air felt cooler here, not with a draft, but with the sensation of stepping into someone else’s quiet. Caleb slowed, listening. The mall’s background hum was still present, yet thinner, as if the building were holding its breath.

### Scene 6 Map Mismatch

He reached a directory kiosk—one of those backlit maps with bright “YOU ARE HERE” arrows. The screen was dark for the night, but the printed map beneath the glass was still visible. Caleb glanced at it out of habit.

The north wing on the map ended at the old department store. A clean line. A final wall.

In front of him, the corridor continued past that point.

It wasn’t subtle. The line of tile stretched on into dimness, the ceiling lights repeating in a pattern that suggested more mall than the map allowed. Caleb blinked, looked down again, then up, waiting for his brain to correct itself.

### Scene 7 Hidden Door and Echo

His radio popped. Not Frankie. A thin burst of breath and a sound like a microphone being moved.

“Frankie?” Caleb asked. He pressed the button, held it down a second too long, then released. The static returned, heavier now, as if it had gained texture.

Something moved near the ceiling—just a flicker, like a light stuttering. Caleb raised his flashlight and followed the beam upward. The fixtures were steady. The only motion was his own hand trembling a little.

He told himself to turn around, to do the sane thing and finish the round where the lights were brighter. His feet didn’t obey right away. Curiosity made a kind of gravity.

A faint sound drifted from ahead. Not footsteps. More like the soft shush of cloth against tile, the whisper of movement that didn’t want to be caught. Caleb’s mouth went dry. He started walking again, slower, careful with each step, as if the floor might punish him for noise.

The corridor widened into a small junction he didn’t recognize, where a service door sat flush with the wall. It was painted the same beige as everything else and should have been forgettable. Caleb stared at it because the sign mounted above the handle looked new.

**SUBLEVEL ACCESS — AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL ONLY**

The mall didn’t have a sublevel. Not according to Frankie. Not according to the map. Caleb leaned closer and saw fresh screws in the sign’s corners, metal bright against the worn paint.

His radio crackled again, and this time the sound carried shape—someone trying to speak through a mouthful of static. Caleb held the device close to his ear, heart thudding hard enough to make his fingertips numb.

A voice slipped through, faint but unmistakable, pitched low as if afraid of being overheard.

It was his own.

“Caleb,” it said, distorted, stretched like tape. “Don’t—don’t open—”

The transmission cut off with a sharp click.

### Scene 8 The Building Awakes

Behind him, in the main concourse, the mall’s public-address system came alive without warning. No preamble. No polite chime.

“Attention Meridian guests,” the recorded woman’s voice said, and for the first time Caleb heard something wrong in it—an inflection that didn’t belong to a loop. “The time is now…”

There was a pause, long enough for Caleb to count two breaths.

“…now.”

The lights above the corridor flickered. The service door’s handle shifted with a soft metallic tick, as if someone on the other side had tested it.

Caleb’s hand tightened around the keyring. The keys bit into his palm. He stood facing the door that shouldn’t exist, listening to the mall’s vast silence rearrange itself around him, and understood with sudden clarity that the building was no longer simply empty.

He told himself to turn around, to do the sane thing and finish the round where the lights were brighter. His feet didn’t obey right away. Curiosity made a kind of gravity.

A faint sound drifted from ahead. Not footsteps. More like the soft shush of cloth against tile, the whisper of movement that didn’t want to be caught. Caleb’s mouth went dry. He started walking again, slower, careful with each step, as if the floor might punish him for noise.

The corridor widened into a small junction he didn’t recognize, where a service door sat flush with the wall. It was painted the same beige as everything else and should have been forgettable. Caleb stared at it because the sign mounted above the handle looked new.
**SUBLEVEL ACCESS — AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL ONLY**
The mall didn’t have a sublevel. Not according to Frankie. Not according to the map. Caleb leaned closer and saw fresh screws in the sign’s corners, metal bright against the worn paint.

His radio crackled again, and this time the sound carried shape—someone trying to speak through a mouthful of static. Caleb held the device close to his ear, heart thudding hard enough to make his fingertips numb.
A voice slipped through, faint but unmistakable, pitched low as if afraid of being overheard.
It was his own.
“Caleb,” it said, distorted, stretched like tape. “Don’t—don’t open—”
The transmission cut off with a sharp click.
Behind him, in the main concourse, the mall’s public-address system came alive without warning. No preamble. No polite chime.
“Attention Meridian guests,” the recorded woman’s voice said, and for the first time Caleb heard something wrong in it—an inflection that didn’t belong to a loop. “The time is now…”
There was a pause, long enough for Caleb to count two breaths.
“…now.”
The lights above the corridor flickered. The service door’s handle shifted with a soft metallic tick, as if someone on the other side had tested it.

Caleb’s hand tightened around the keyring. The keys bit into his palm. He stood facing the door that shouldn’t exist, listening to the mall’s vast silence rearrange itself around him, and understood with sudden clarity that the building was no longer simply empty.

story.value:
# After Hours at Meridian Mall

## Chapter One: Closing Time

The last shoppers drifted toward the exits like sleepwalkers being gently steered. Plastic bags swung at their sides, their faces pale under fluorescent light that made everyone look a little unfinished. A janitor pushed a wide mop bucket through the concourse, its wheels squealing in brief protest before settling back into the mall’s steady hum.

Caleb Shaw watched the flow from behind the security desk near the central fountain, where coins glinted in shallow water that hadn’t been cleaned in weeks. He wore the navy blazer the job required, but the tie was already loosened, and his badge sat slightly crooked from habit. New uniforms always felt like someone else’s skin.

“Ten minutes,” his supervisor said, voice crackling through the handheld radio. Frankie had a smoker’s rasp and the tone of a man who’d seen the same night repeat itself for years. “Once the gates come down, it’s you and the cameras.”

Caleb lifted the radio closer. “Copy,” he said. He tried to sound steady, tried to sound like this wasn’t his first solo night at Meridian. He’d worked smaller places—warehouse lots, a shuttered office building—nothing with this many corridors, this many blank storefronts, this much polished space designed for crowds.

Meridian Mall sat on the edge of town, built during the optimistic years when people thought indoor plazas could replace main streets. In daylight it felt ordinary, even cheerful in a tired way. At night, it became a maze of glossy tiles and reflective glass, a place that seemed to wait for something to return.

Overhead, the public-address system cleared its throat with a soft pop. “Attention Meridian guests,” the recorded voice said, warm and artificial. “The time is now nine-fifty. Meridian Mall will be closing in ten minutes. Please make your final selections and proceed to the nearest exit.”

The voice belonged to a woman Caleb had never met and yet heard again and again. Tonight, the recording sounded slightly slower, as if the tape were dragging. He told himself it was the speakers.

The main doors slid shut in pairs as the last people left, leaving only the dull, private click of locks. One by one, store shutters rolled down like metal eyelids. The concourse dimmed as kiosks and neon signs switched off, their colors collapsing into the gray-green of emergency lighting.

Caleb did his first round the way Frankie had taught him: clockwise, main level first, then upper. He carried a keyring heavy enough to bruise a pocket, a small flashlight, and a clipboard with a checklist that felt like a joke in a place this big. The mall had cameras, but patrols were still expected—some old philosophy of security that believed presence mattered.

His shoes made a soft tack-tack on tile. It echoed longer than it should have, the sound traveling ahead of him like an announcement. He passed the closed arcade, its gate latticed and dark, the silhouettes of machines inside forming a little city of sleeping screens. In the food court, chairs were stacked on tables like folded insects, and the smell of fryer oil clung to the air as if it had seeped into the walls.

Near the fountain, Caleb paused to watch the water. The pumps had been turned down for the night, but the surface still trembled as if something beneath it was breathing. He leaned closer, then straightened, annoyed with himself. Fluorescent light played tricks on moving water. That was all.

His radio hissed once, then went quiet. He glanced at it, thumbed the button, and heard only static. Frankie had warned him the building ate signals in certain spots. “Dead zones,” he’d called them, with a half grin that didn’t reach his eyes.

At the far end of the concourse, the corridor to the north wing waited in shadow. Caleb had seen it during the day—anchor stores, a long line of boutiques, a service hallway behind them. Now, with the lights reduced, that corridor looked longer, narrower, more deliberate.

Frankie’s voice came back in Caleb’s memory: *Don’t linger up there. North wing sensors are finicky. Doors stick. If something pings, check it and move on.* He’d said it like advice. He’d looked at Caleb like a warning.

Caleb started down the corridor anyway, telling himself a job was a job. He swept his flashlight beam across store gates, over mannequins frozen in bright outfits that made no sense without customers. Their faces were blank ovals. Their hands held invisible waists. The beam caught them in slices, making them appear to turn when he moved.

Halfway down, his gut tightened for no good reason. The air felt cooler here, not with a draft, but with the sensation of stepping into someone else’s quiet. Caleb slowed, listening. The mall’s background hum was still present, yet thinner, as if the building were holding its breath.

He reached a directory kiosk—one of those backlit maps with bright “YOU ARE HERE” arrows. The screen was dark for the night, but the printed map beneath the glass was still visible. Caleb glanced at it out of habit.

The north wing on the map ended at the old department store. A clean line. A final wall.

In front of him, the corridor continued past that point.

It wasn’t subtle. The line of tile stretched on into dimness, the ceiling lights repeating in a pattern that suggested more mall than the map allowed. Caleb blinked, looked down again, then up, waiting for his brain to correct itself.

His radio popped. Not Frankie. A thin burst of breath and a sound like a microphone being moved.

“Frankie?” Caleb asked. He pressed the button, held it down a second too long, then released. The static returned, heavier now, as if it had gained texture.

Something moved near the ceiling—just a flicker, like a light stuttering. Caleb raised his flashlight and followed the beam upward. The fixtures were steady. The only motion was his own hand trembling a little.

He told himself to turn around, to do the sane thing and finish the round where the lights were brighter. His feet didn’t obey right away. Curiosity made a kind of gravity.

A faint sound drifted from ahead. Not footsteps. More like the soft shush of cloth against tile, the whisper of movement that didn’t want to be caught. Caleb’s mouth went dry. He started walking again, slower, careful with each step, as if the floor might punish him for noise.

The corridor widened into a small junction he didn’t recognize, where a service door sat flush with the wall. It was painted the same beige as everything else and should have been forgettable. Caleb stared at it because the sign mounted above the handle looked new.

**SUBLEVEL ACCESS — AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL ONLY**

The mall didn’t have a sublevel. Not according to Frankie. Not according to the map. Caleb leaned closer and saw fresh screws in the sign’s corners, metal bright against the worn paint.

His radio crackled again, and this time the sound carried shape—someone trying to speak through a mouthful of static. Caleb held the device close to his ear, heart thudding hard enough to make his fingertips numb.

A voice slipped through, faint but unmistakable, pitched low as if afraid of being overheard.

It was his own.

“Caleb,” it said, distorted, stretched like tape. “Don’t—don’t open—”

The transmission cut off with a sharp click.

Behind him, in the main concourse, the mall’s public-address system came alive without warning. No preamble. No polite chime.

“Attention Meridian guests,” the recorded woman’s voice said, and for the first time Caleb heard something wrong in it—an inflection that didn’t belong to a loop. “The time is now…”

There was a pause, long enough for Caleb to count two breaths.

“…now.”

The lights above the corridor flickered. The service door’s handle shifted with a soft metallic tick, as if someone on the other side had tested it.

Caleb’s hand tightened around the keyring. The keys bit into his palm. He stood facing the door that shouldn’t exist, listening to the mall’s vast silence rearrange itself around him, and understood with sudden clarity that the building was no longer simply empty.
